[09:24] Cool. Okay, so yes, you guys are absolutely right. We've covered a bunch of stuff last time case, right? Could be. But again, those are all affinity biases, right? Biases.

 

[09:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Affinity Bias - Birds of a Feather Flock Together"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Imagem de pavões coloridos e um pato comum, representando diferenças e preconceitos; quote "I might be different, but I have the skills you need"

 

 

[09:38] So very simply put, an affinity bias is the unconscious tendency to get along with others who are like us. That's just simple, right? You look at the bottom, the quote, birds of a feather flock together. It cannot be any truer than that.

 

[09:54] But look at the poor duck. I might be different, but I have the skills you need, right? Poor duck, right? I guess these are what, peacocks? They look like peacocks, right? They have all nice little tails, they should be colorful, and the poor little duck has no fancy tail, right, to show off.

 

[10:12] But the duck has different skills. Think about it. What can a duck do that peacocks can't do? I know a lot they can actually do, right? Fly, swim, yeah, absolutely, absolutely.

 

[10:25] So now, this is something I don't think is like mind-blowing, it's not rocket science. I think we all can agree, we all know that it is easy to socialize, spend time, make friends, get along with others who are similar in nature.

 

[10:40] This means, you know, culture, even age, demographics, gender, nationality, ethnicity, right? It's all very, very common, right? It's all something that we are aware of.

 

[10:52] It does require more effort. Wouldn't you agree, guys, that if we wanted diversity to our friend group or to our workplace, it does require more effort because, you know, maybe these people have a different language, they have a different culture, different beliefs.

 

[11:08] Like, a very perfect example that I had growing up, well, you know, when I moved from, this is just a funny example, it's not really affinity bias, but it goes to culture, right? It's like moving from Malaysia to the US, you know, in Malaysia, I think all of my Asian brothers and sisters can agree with me, you can holler at me, but in Asia, when you go home, you don't really, you know, you don't keep your shoes, you don't keep your shoes on. Let's put it that way, right? You take your shoes off.

 

[11:35] You step in the front door, you take your shoes outside the front door, that's just a thing. But, you know, coming to the US, like a lot of my friends that I went to their house, I was really shocked in the beginning when they told me to keep my shoes on, really. Like, honest to God, I was like, whoa, you guys keep your shoes on, right?

 

[11:52] And that's nothing wrong with that. It's just, it's just, it's just, it's just what it is, right? It's just the culture is different, right? So Christy, you got something to add to this, go ahead.

 

[12:03] Yeah, this is something that you can also find really easily for people in the workplace. Because if you've noticed, there are like little clicks or the famous phrase, the grapevine. The grapevine is normally people who are already together just spreading things.

 

[12:19] Like people that have a common hatred for their boss. Well, they're going to be together. And then everybody who's like, oh, I think he's totally fair, you know, everything's fine. Well, now they're in a separate group.

 

[12:30] Now nobody wants to talk to them because now they're like, well, if we talk to you, it's not that we necessarily don't like you, but you know, you're going to go against my beliefs. You're going to try to convince me otherwise. And then we're going to get into a fight and then I'm not going to like you anyway. Because now I think you can't get along with people. So that's a very common one.

 

[12:48] Yeah, you're right, Christy. And we're going to look at an example as why this is so detrimental as a salesperson. Or even a closer in general.

 

[12:57] I'll give you guys an example and then James and Christy, feel free to chime in. But I've seen this a lot in the workplace where we have recruiting, right? The manager recruits closers. Can you guys agree? That's like a very common task of our manager, right? A manager helps with recruiting. Finding good talent, hiring good talent, onboarding good talent.

 

[13:18] Now imagine this, right? Imagine if a manager, his or her affinity bias was so strong that he or she only recruited people that looked like him or her. Right? Now this could be race, this could be gender, this could be even language.

 

[13:37] So that now that, you know, you might laugh, but that's actually a real thing. Now look at all the cons, all the disadvantages you would get from not having diversity.

 

[13:48] First of all, right? Where is the value of having people that could close in multiple languages? Well, that's completely out the window because I want my whole sales team to be only English-speaking.

 

[14:00] Where is the diversity of my sales team? Let's say they are, you know, door-to-door or in-person sales and they have to meet minorities, they have to meet people of different cultures, traditions, but your entire team is just one culture or one ethnicity, right? Where is that? Then you lose all of that, right?

 

[14:18] But again, it might sound, it might seem like it's like common sense or, you know, something that we all should know. But I can assure you, if we don't keep this top of mind, it is very easy to let it creep up on us. That makes it unconscious, right?

 

[14:34] So when you're hiring, like for myself or for James and Christy, really, even for myself, I got to keep top of mind. You know what? Just because I know this guy or girl from Malaysia or this guy or girl, you know, speaks whatever, my language, right? It doesn't mean he or she is a good fit for my team.

 

[14:51] Or it doesn't make he or she a better closer just because he or she is from the country I was born in, right? That's not a thing, right? It could be true, but that's not a fact unless verified.

 

[15:03] So this is something we really want to keep top of mind, right? Now, James and Christy, do you guys have any other examples of this before we move on?

 

[15:12] It's a great point, you know. You want to have it as an inclusive workplace as well, too, because, you know, having that type of mindset that's there, disastrous.

 

[15:22] Yeah, the other thing as well is that, like, when we're hiring as well, like, I have my own way to close, Joel has his own way, James has his own way. If I'm going to hire someone only because they use the Columbo technique, you use the Columbo? Awesome. Get on my team.

 

[15:36] That's not going to be very helpful because, you know, all different prospects close different ways as far as what their needs are. So even when I'm having to do a role play, I'm like, okay, can that work? Is it my preference? No. But can somebody close this way and be effective? That's the one thing that we have to definitely keep in mind as well.

 

[15:54] Yeah, that's very well said, Christy. I love that. Okay, awesome.

 

[15:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Confirmation Bias"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Imagem mostrando pessoa olhando através de lente distorcida, vendo apenas fatos que confirmam suas crenças pré-existentes; "The Truth" vs "What I See"

 

 

[16:00] So the next one, I think a lot of us may, well, you guys, we will know what this is when it's explained, but the actual term for it is confirmation bias.

 

[16:10] So that's really, you know, you can look at this image, right? It's really clear and obvious. The objective, the facts, right? The truth is sort of like, yeah, it's there, but I really only see what really confirms my beliefs.

 

[16:24] I see some facts, but I don't see all the facts because I only want to see what I want to see. I want to believe what I was told when I was young that I believe, right? I want to see what I learned growing up in school that was the way. So I only want to see that. So I confirm all my beliefs. So that's confirmation bias.

 

[16:44] We're actually going to play a pretty fun game right now. We're going to play a pretty fun game. And if some of you have seen this test or this game, don't spoil it for everyone else. If you know the answer to it, don't spoil it.

 

[16:57] But I'm going to try to play this with the hundreds of people here in Discord. Actually, depending on how many of you chime in, we'll try to keep it to the couple tens of people here, right?

 

[17:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Confirmation Bias Game - Number Sequence"

 

NÚMEROS: 10, 15, 20

 

VISUAL: Jogo interativo, sequência numérica inicial para teste de viés de confirmação

 

 

[17:10] So here's how this game works. I'm going to give you three numbers. I'm going to say three numbers, and you are going to repeat back in Discord three numbers. Back to me. And you will try to basically find out what the rule is, what the rule of my numbers are. What kind of rule I'm using, okay?

 

[17:30] Now, I will tell you whether your numbers are following my rule or not following my rule. If they are following my rule, I'll say yes, they follow my rule. If they don't follow my rule, I'll tell you that.

 

[17:42] And then if you think you've got the rule, I want you to share. If you think you've got the rule, hold it to yourself first because I want everyone to join to play. And then if you've got the rule, I will ask, who thinks they have the rule? And you can say, yeah, I have the rule.

 

[17:58] Then I'll ask you, what is the rule? And you can tell me, okay? If the rule is not correct, I'll tell you it's not correct. And if the rule is correct, then we're done because everyone's going to know what the rule is, okay?

 

[18:11] Okay, are you guys ready? If you're ready on Discord, say, I'm ready, let's play. Okay, here are the three numbers. Ready? 10, 15, 20.

 

[18:28] [Jogo interativo continua com participação da audiência no Discord]

 

[19:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Confirmation Bias in Sales Management"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Exemplo de viés de confirmação aplicado ao ambiente de vendas, impacto nas decisões gerenciais

 

 

[20:15] What's going on? When I took them into an individual, well first I put them in the group and I was like, you know, hey what's going on? Everybody's like, I don't know, I don't know what's going on.

 

[20:26] So I took them as kind of a one-on-one. Like, okay, what's going on? What I was hearing is, well so and so is doing this. So why should I do what you're asking when everybody else is doing this?

 

[20:38] So the fact that everybody else was being a low performer, it could have just been because they had a bad week, right? And everybody is now following that example just because that person had a bad week.

 

[20:50] Now they think it's okay and now it comes back to, okay, we have to change that because that is not a bias that we can have in our team.

 

[21:00] And this is not only, like Chris did that, so thanks for saying that. That's so true. I remember that situation that you mentioned to me as well. And this doesn't just affect closers, right? It affects the managers too, even the directors, even like, you know, everyone, anyone, CEOs, COOs, C-level executives, everyone has biases, right?

 

[21:20] We just got to be self-aware and understand and respect the fact that we have them. It's not about being, you know, I'm going to try and eliminate all the biases from my life because I can guarantee you, you can never get rid of biases, right? You will always have some form of bias, right, in your life.

 

[21:38] So it's about being self-aware, being cognizant, and being just understanding to know, you know what? I'm recruiting right now. I'm talking to my team right now. Oh, this bias has just popped up. Let me not have that control, my conversation, right?

 

[21:54] From a manager's perspective, what was the process to determine those bias? Chris, do you want to answer Justin's question? And then we'll go to James.

 

[22:04] Yeah, that's a very good question. The main thing that you have to do is first put them in two different environments. So the first environment I put them in is as a group. Like, hey guys, you know, I noticed over the past, like, two weeks, this is what's happening.

 

[22:20] Giving them the data that shows why it's a concern. See what they all say together. When the first person speaks up, they go, I don't know. I mean, I didn't really change anything. Oh, me neither.

 

[22:32] Well, we know that something changed because when they're like, oh, I used to do 40 calls in a day. Now there's 20. Yeah, something happened.

 

[22:40] So then it's like, okay, so now we have to figure out if everybody's saying, oh, nothing really changed or I had one bad day, but this went over a two-week span. Now I got to go one by one. Do the answers change?

 

[22:54] Similar to what Joel was saying that they did in this experiment is then you have to see, is it an individual thing or is it a group thing? Now some of them actually said, yeah, nothing really changed. You know, maybe I can do some tweaks here and there to get it back up, but, you know, nothing huge changed, which is then fine.

 

[23:11] Then it's like, okay, yours wasn't a bias. Yours is legitimately this is what happened. But when you then talk to others, you find that, oh, this percentage actually had that bias.

 

[23:22] So it's taking it to different environments, comparing the answers, and making an educated decision what it is so then that way you can do the correct way to fix it and to get it back on track.

 

[23:34] All right, James, you got an example?

 

[23:37] Yeah, so this one actually comes down to being the observer. And when it comes down to other individuals, say like if you have a little bit of a dry spell, it's the first couple days of the month and you see the first person get the sale and you see they do it in a particular way and fashion and you try to mimic or you try to copy that.

 

[23:58] And we all know, you know, in closing, we have to be like water. And if we try to conform in a particular way or fashion, if we're so rigid, we actually lose out on being able to discover what the true pain point is or trying to figure out what their needs truly are.

 

[24:15] If we're looking in one way or fashion and not being like water and trying to mimic someone else's closing skill, we're lying to ourselves, not being the high-ticket closer.

 

[24:26] So well put there, James. Awesome. All right, so the last effect or the last bias we're going to cover tonight is the halo or the horns effect. The halo or the horns effect.

 

[24:39] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Halo Effect vs Horns Effect"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando dois eixos: Facts (You are NOT good / You ARE good) vs Referee thinks (You ARE good / You are NOT good); representação visual de julgamento baseado em impressões vs realidade

 

 

[24:42] Now, this one is something that is, I think, something we're all very aware of. And we see this day to day, if not all the time, right, happening. And this is where, you know, people get really frustrated. They always say things like, oh, he or she is so unfair. My boss is so, you know, has favoritism. There's politics. You know, everything is so unfair, right? This is where this comes from, okay?

 

[25:08] So let's look at the left, the y-axis, the fact. The fact is, on the bottom, you are not good. Let's just say, right, me, me, I as a closer, I'm not good. I'm not good at closing. Okay, that's a fact. I'm not good at closing.

 

[25:24] Now, you look at the top, the x-axis, the referee, the evaluator, the sales manager, Christy James. Now, if Christy or James thinks I am good because they know me for two years now, right, they've known me, I'm their friend, I'm their buddy, we hang out after school, you know, I went to, like, I don't know, restaurants with them and bars with them.

 

[25:46] Oh, yeah, you know, Joel, you're a great closer. You're amazing closer, right? But the fact is, I'm not good.

 

[25:53] And then on the flip side, right, you are good. You are a good closer, right, the top one. I'm good. I'm actually a solid closer. But then, you know, the manager, the valid referee thinks I am not good and that's the horns effect, right?

 

[26:09] Horns effect maybe James saw me, I don't know, doing some, like, he saw me doing something that he didn't think it was good, like, a week ago. And I went in for, you know, a recruiting session and he wanted to hire me. And because of what he saw me do, that immediately triggered, you know what, Joel is not good just because of that one incident.

 

[26:30] Because I saw him do that one thing, therefore Joel's closing skill is not good, which is not true, right? But it's just the horns effect affected James' decision making.

 

[26:41] And, again, the converse was the halo effect. Just because James knew me from school, we were college friends, high school friends, whatever, I actually suck as a closer, but because he likes me, he hired me as this sales rep.

 

[26:56] And guess what's worse? Even worse, he gave me more opportunities than everyone else on the team. And that's where you have all the favoritism, bias, right? My boss is unfair, right? They only like people they know. And this is reality. This is true.

 

[27:13] So this is called the halo or the horns effect. And you can look at the arrow, the blue and green. The halo effect is usually gives a positive impression. Now this guy or girl has higher equity. Now I trust them more. They have better value just because they took out, they bought me coffee last week.

 

[27:33] You might laugh, but that's a real thing, right? Hey, you know, Felix bought me coffee. Therefore, he's a better closer. You might think it's funny, but there are people that actually conclude that. And it's not their fault. It's really an unconscious bias, right? Which they should then be aware of to fix.

 

[27:52] Or, you know, horn effect, negative impression, lower equity, lesser value, low trust. Guess what? James did not buy me a coffee when everyone did. And then I feel, oh, James, you must be a bad closer. Right, James? You didn't buy me coffee. Therefore, you're a bad closer. Right?

 

[28:09] And I'm your manager, so yeah, you must be a bad closer. And that's what it is. And again, it's a very simplistic example, but this is what you see in the workplace and what we see every day in the sales management world. Right? And no, don't have to buy me coffee.

 

[28:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Halo/Horns Effect Definition"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Definição formal do efeito halo/chifres, impacto nas percepções e decisões gerenciais

 

 

[28:28] So, yeah, the halo or horns effect is where a person's impression of another can substantially influence one's thoughts and feelings about that person.

 

[28:38] Christy, James, before we conclude the biases section tonight, any last words, any examples to add for the halo and the horns?

 

[28:47] I want to add also kind of like the, there's one that's kind of a little subtle with this one as well. It is the person's interpretation of the halo and horn as well. Because let's say that, for an example, that, okay, Joel, I only bought you coffee.

 

[29:03] Well, maybe the other person didn't know that we actually had a contest going on. And really, he earned that coffee. But now the other person also perceives that, oh, well, she must really like him because, you know, she only bought him coffee.

 

[29:17] Now that's also on a managerial part that, you know, I do have to let the team know, like all the challenges and stuff like that. So there does come responsibility with that. But there is this interpretation you can get as well from it. So it could be done unintentionally as well.

 

[29:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Goal Setting Frameworks: OKRs vs KPIs vs MTOs"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Comparação de três sistemas de definição de metas, quando usar cada um

 

 

[29:38] And closing people into Cop Academy, right? Which is a great program. If you haven't checked it out, by the way, here to plug Cop Academy, right? Drop the checkout link, you know, in the chat. I'm joking, guys.

 

[29:51] Not to sidetrack us, but let's say our project is closing people into Cop Academy. Well, if it's already going on, I would create a KPI for that, right? The KPI could be super straightforward. Every week, every month, Christy, your team is closing Cop Academy. We've got to hit at least 50 Cop Academy sales a month.

 

[30:11] That's just a simple key performance indicator, right? That's it. That's a KPI, right? It's not even an objective. You could say, well, the objective is 50, and the key result, here's how I'm going to get to 50. You could do that, but that's too complicated. You know what I mean? You don't have to go into that detail.

 

[30:30] Versus if I said, okay, here's my OKRs. Here's where I do it. When I have a large vision or I want to change my overall direction, okay?

 

[30:41] So let me give you an example. Let's say the example was closing, also closing into Cop Academy. But here's the difference. Instead of, look, we say, you know, maybe Sifu Dan or we say, the executive team says, you know what? We want to change the direction of closing Cop Academy sales.

 

[31:00] We want to now add outbound sales to Cop Academy, meaning we want to have SDRs do quote outreach to sell people to Cop Academy. We're not just going to give you inbound leads. We're going to give you inbound leads and outbound leads. You got to go get outbound.

 

[31:17] So Christy, your job is now to manage outbound and inbound Cop Academy sales. Now in this case, I would say, you know what? Let's look at this from the OKR model, right?

 

[31:29] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "OKR Example: Cop Academy Sales"

 

NÚMEROS: 50 sales/month total, 25 inbound, 25 outbound, 100 leads/week, 50 calls/day, 100 emails/week

 

VISUAL: Breakdown detalhado de OKR com objetivo principal e key results específicos para inbound e outbound

 

 

[31:31] Okay, here's why. Here's why I do OKR model. And I'm going to actually type in Discord, okay? So the objective would be still the same, 50 Cop Academy sales a month, okay?

 

[31:44] But my key result number one, let's just say, keep it simple, 25 sales from inbound sales team. That's my KR1. My key result two, as you can guess, is 25 sales from outbound sales team, right? That would be my OKR.

 

[32:02] And this is very simplified. It doesn't really look like this. But even within the key result, I have broken it down into how does the inbound sales team operate? That means, is it 100 leads a week for the inbound sales team?

 

[32:18] And for key result number two, is it 50 phone calls a day? I don't know. Is it, I don't know, 100 emails a week? Whatever, right? I break down the new objective into much more detail, right?

 

[32:32] So can you see the difference between KPIs and OKRs and when we use them, right? And of course, you know, we could say MTOs. If we want to keep it extremely simple, right? Christy, it's very simple. Your team got to get 30 minimum, target is 50, and outrageous is 75.

 

[32:52] We don't care where the breakdown comes from. I don't care if you got 49 inbound sales and one outbound sale. It's just an MTO, right? That's the MTO. Just go for that, right?

 

[33:04] So three different ways to measure goals, OKRs, KPIs, MTOs. I wouldn't, so to answer the question, do we mix it? I wouldn't necessarily mix them. You could mix them in the company for sure, right? You could mix them in a company for sure.

 

[33:22] However, if you have like a project, you have something you're already working on, something you want to start brand new, keep one type of measurement for your team because otherwise you keep switching.

 

[33:34] Imagine this. You're the closer. One week, Christy tells you we're doing MTOs. Next week, she tells you we're doing OKRs. The week after, she tells you we're doing KPIs. You know, then what, right?

 

[33:46] It's like confusion just leads to no action. And if you're confused, a confused mind doesn't do anything, right? So that I want to talk about. I want to clarify that really quick. And then I would look at some examples, right? Christy?

 

[34:01] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "OKR Example: Achieving Physical Sustainability"

 

NÚMEROS: 11% to 5% variance, 95% capital project dollars, 95% grant dollars

 

VISUAL: Exemplo real de OKR corporativo com objetivo financeiro e três key results específicos com métricas percentuais

 

 

[34:03] Yep. So this is one example of the OKRs, which Joel kind of already did a really good job of explaining it, that the objective is something that your company wants to achieve. And you can even use OKRs in your own life as well if you wanted to put a personal spin on it.

 

[34:19] So this is achieving physical sustainability, which means to, for like taxes and, you know, different things like that for the company, right? Their financial status. So it's like, OK, but as you can see, it's very generic. It's very generic, but it doesn't tell you how you're going to get there. It just tells you what you want to achieve, which is great.

 

[34:38] OK, this is what we're achieving. So then for our key results, reduce the general fund budget variance from 11 to 5%. OK, then the next thing is spend 95% of authorized capital project dollars by the end of the fiscal year and then spend 95% of grant dollars for grants from prior fiscal year.

 

[35:00] So it comes up with an action plan where as then MTOs are not as specific. So we will go into that in a couple of slides here. But that's why I love OKRs. It starts very generic. It goes step by step, gives the additional clarity. So there is no confusion. There is none at all.

 

[35:21] Because if I say, OK, achieve 50 sales using Joel's example, like you said, it could be anything, inbound, outbound. It could just be, you know, completely random sales. It's like, where did they come from? Maybe they came from marketing.

 

[35:34] But when we have our key results, it actually says, oh, OK, well, if we can get even 25 from inbound, we get then 25 from outbound, we make it. And then you break it down from there as well.

 

[35:46] And again, right, the objective is so straightforward. We just want to achieve fiscal sustainability, and we're going to do that by having these three KRs. There you go.

[39:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MTO Framework (Minimum, Target, Outrageous)"

 

NÚMEROS: 30 minimum, 35 target, 34-35 actual performance range

 

VISUAL: Framework de três níveis de metas, estratégia de definição de objetivos com margem de segurança

 

 

[39:10] With this, the reason why I go to my team and I say to them, okay guys, we need a minimum of 30, but our target is 35. Because if they come to me and go, Christy, I know we wanted 35. We barely hit 30. Like, I don't even know if we can hit 32.

 

[39:28] And then I can go back to them and go, okay guys, you know, we may not have hit 35. That's where we want it to go. But let's push for extra two sales. Let's get 34. Let's get close.

 

[39:40] Because now their mindset is, if I have to hit 35, I come up a little short. The goal that's actually in place behind the scenes has already been met. So then I can praise them that, you know, guys, you hit your 30. That's good. We hit the first metric.

 

[39:56] But we didn't get to target. Let's coach on why. Let's keep improving. But giving them that praise of we still hit the minimum target. We still hit our goal on that level. So next time we're going to improve and we're going to hit target next time.

 

[40:12] Which then if you think about it, let's say they did the opposite. They did really freaking well. They actually hit their target, 35. And they're like, they're thinking, oh my God, we barely got there. That was a lot. Holy crap.

 

[40:26] Give them that talk again. Praise them for doing such a wonderful job. Creating that culture. I go back to Joel and I say, hey Joel, we got 35. Do you think Joel will be happy or just be like, why couldn't you do better? Like what response do you think I would get from Joel if I came to him and said we did 35?

 

[40:45] Now we go for 100, Christine, one week. Let's do it. That's really how it rolls in TDL. And then I say, okay, 100 is minimum. Now we're doing 150 for our target. It always goes like that. Absolutely.

[41:52] It's just a very distinct difference. People are typing. Type, type, type. Not recognize talent. Okay, not that. There's a difference between top down and bottom up.

 

[42:05] Come on, who wasn't you, Ultra HTC, like three weeks ago? I feel like you guys should know. This should be something ingrained in your minds by now. Top down versus bottom up management styles.

 

[42:18] Yeah, Adam, top down is oppressive. Yes, Adam, Bogdan's got it. Yes, fantastic. So, yeah, top down is everything comes from the top. You guys got to get 50 sales or dead. You guys got to do this. You got to do that. I'm the boss, so you guys do this, right? That's top down.

 

[42:38] It could be like that, right? It's not always like that. It could be like that. Bottom up is, hey, guys, I know you guys have a lot of work and a lot of targets and products and stuff like that. But for Copy Academy sales, what is the realistic number that you think you could get?

 

[42:56] Now, I'm talking to the SWAT team, right, the SWAT team. Then I go to the AEs. Okay, AEs, how many, or the sharpshooters, how many sales do you think you guys could get?

 

[43:08] And then I collate all the numbers and I give them a number to, let's say that was Christy, and Christy gives the number to me. Joel, I spoke to my team, and after reviewing with them, we could get up to 25 sales this week after speaking to my team, right?

 

[43:24] That's a very big difference, right? One is I go to Christy, I say, Christy, we need 25 sales, get it done. The other one is Christy spends some time and spoke to both teams of closers and go, we feel with our ability and our current capacity, we can get 25 sales, no problem.

 

[43:42] Or we might not. We think 25 sales is too high. We can only do 15. And then Christy and I will further negotiate, right? And then I would negotiate with Sifu. That's how it is.

[44:12] No pop quiz without a prize. Hey, you win the most attentive in class tonight, John. Okay, yeah, you're right. No, it usually is top-down. MTOs are usually top-down, typically, because targets are set. It comes from the top.

 

[44:28] Christy doesn't really go talk to the closers and decide an MTO. Sifu, the team, we just say, you know what? We've got to hit 5, 10, 15, get it done. We do it, or we're not going to have revenue. We're not going to make it this month. We've got to do it, right?

 

[44:44] Now, let me ask you guys this. What about OKRs? OKRs are top-down or bottom-up? This is a very key distinction. It's extremely, extremely key.

 

[44:56] Yeah, you guys are right. It's bottom-up. Extremely bottom-up. In fact, you could never do an OKR. Oh, you could, but it will never be effective if you did an OKR top-down.

 

[45:10] OKRs are always bottom-up, and that's the beauty of OKRs. That's the other thing I want to add, right? But OKRs, you actually take into consideration what your team could do, right? What's realistic? What's achievable?

 

[45:24] And then aim for maybe stretch it a little bit. Stretch it a little bit, but then that's your OKR, right? It's oftentimes very bottom-up.

 

[45:34] And then where the beauty comes in is usually the negotiation piece between, like, for example, myself, right, the director with Sifu, right? Because, for example, Sifu has an OKR, and then, you know, Sifu might give us an OKR of we want to get X number of sales.

 

[45:50] I will talk to Christy. Hey, Christy, you know, Sifu wants us to hit this number. But let's go talk to the closers first, right, Christy? We've done that many times before, right?

 

[46:02] And then, yeah, absolutely. Yeah, Christy goes talk to the closers, and Christy comes back, and Christy says, well, Joel, I spoke to them. [46:20] Guess what? I don't confirm the OKRs at that time. I go back to Sifu, and I go, hey, you know, Dan or Sifu Dan, just spoke to our team. Based on X, Y, and Z, and by the way, we never make this sound like excuses, right? We never say excuses.

 

[46:36] Like, oh, no, my team, we can't do that. We're too busy, right? You know, realistically we're working on this project, and we want to always give the best to the company, and I know you want to get to 100 sales, but we can actually look at that more realistically next quarter.

 

[46:52] This quarter we can actually look at 75. How does that sound? Guess what, guys? What is that called? What is that called? What is the phrase, how does that sound? Where does that come from, right?

 

[47:06] It comes from, come on, guys, we should all know this answer. Come on. It's a one-word answer. Negotiation also known as closing. It's called closing. Yes.

[48:54] Like a platoon sergeant, private, things like that. Like most of them, I mean, in the military, they could take it, right? They just do it. They have to, there's no choice.

 

[49:06] But people I work with, some of them come and go all the time, just go. They just go, they just can't take my style. And by the way, as a manager, as a director, we've got to be okay with that, you know?

 

[49:20] Like we've got to accept that. You know, we've got to accept that we're not a cup of tea for everyone, right? In fact, I don't want to be a cup of tea for everyone because if I am, then am I being too pleasing, right?

[49:48] So a lot of, it's hard because sometimes you do it out of care, but the person you're doing it to might think you're being very mean, being rude, right? [50:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "12-Month Manager Development Rule"

 

NÚMEROS: 12 months maximum tenure in same position, 1 month minimum before termination decision

 

VISUAL: Filosofia de desenvolvimento acelerado, duas opções após 12 meses: promoção ou saída

 

 

[50:26] And usually people don't last that long, right? But then, you know, when you last that long, then there is light at the end of the tunnel, right? Because here's my belief. I think I mentioned this to, maybe I mentioned this to you guys before on class, but here's my belief in my style is that if I get a new manager under my team and Christy, Felix, and James has heard this many times, you have 12 months, 12 months and you're gone, right?

 

[50:50] And you can be gone two ways. One, gone, you get promoted, you do better things, you move to a better company, whatever, you move to a bigger place, you do something else, fantastic. So proud of you, I love you.

 

[51:04] The other way you could be gone is you suck so bad, you're just done. Like you don't even make 12 months. You make one month and you're so bad, you're just fired, right?

 

[51:14] But I never wanna keep people for more than 12 months because I always wanna see them develop into something better. I always feel like if, and this is where the dependency come in, right?

 

[51:26] And this is another, it's not on the slides, but it's a managerial thing that I've learned is that if I always rely on the same people, you know, I could easily do this. I could easily say, you know what? James, Christy, Felix, I've trained you guys for one year, I put in my blood, sweat, and tears, I want you guys to stay with me for five years in the same position.

 

[51:46] Let me ask you guys this, would that be fair? Would that be fair for Felix, James, and Christy at all, right? No, it's not. Even though I put in my blood, sweat, and tears, I'm still stifling their performance.

 

[52:00] And you know what? You know what? The worst part about that is only insecure, I would say insecure and selfish, you know, directors and managers think that way because I have such good people under me, I don't wanna lose them.

 

[52:16] It's so much work to train another Felix or James or Christy, you know? There's so much work trying to have, oh, I gotta find another Christy, I gotta spend 12 months, oh my God, I don't have time for that, I'm gonna, I might lose my directive position, what if the people I bring are not good?

 

[52:32] Like, those are insecurities, right? Like, if you know you're good, you're not worried about helping people move up. And Christy's so right, right? You leverage them to help.

 

[52:44] Then it's like leadership experience for Christy and Felix and James. Can you find someone to groom and to nurture so they can become, they can replace you?

 

[52:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Replacement Rule: Develop Your Successor"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Filosofia de sucessão: não pode ser promovido sem desenvolver substituto, prova de capacidade de desenvolvimento de talentos

 

 

[52:58] Because that's my other rule. My other rule is this. You don't move to become a sales manager unless, I mean, sorry, you don't move away from your position unless you can find a replacement. Why? Why?

 

[53:12] Because I wanna see that you could develop people. I wanna see that you can actually take someone, break them down, help them become better and put them in a better spot so you can leave your position and have them take it over.

 

[53:26] So it does two things, right? One, it's not empty. And two, the manager, Christy, Felix, and James has shown that they can develop other people.

 

[53:36] Now, and this is the big part. I always want my team to succeed. I wanna prepare them for a future position, right? Whatever it may be, right? Whatever it may be, up the company, somewhere else, doesn't matter. So I want them to move on and do something better than someone else comes in.

 

[53:54] Yeah, exactly, Justin. Capable of a role next chapter, exactly. And then, yeah, so that's sort of my mantra of management, right?

[57:00] So we have CRM, results. No, no, it's not CRM, not results. CRM holds these metrics. Numbers game. Cannot improve if it's not measured. Oh, I like John's answer. Yeah. I like Adam's answer too. Getting closer, getting closer. Conversions, yes.

 

[57:20] So it's important to know how many leads we have because this is going to actually tell us exactly how many people are we able to reach out to. Because let's say a closer has just 10 leads. First week on, they have 10 leads. They don't get one sale. Well, am I going to be surprised? Not really.

 

[57:38] You only have 10 leads. You can only contact them so many times before they start thinking that you're just spamming them. All right? And we don't want to do that.

 

[57:48] But if a person has, let's say, 2,000 leads, no sales, and it's been over a month, that tells me there's something wrong. There's something wrong with the process they have set up. There's something wrong with maybe the closing. There's something else wrong.

[58:30] That's a good point, actually, when using the data. Just to kind of touch base on that from the last slide. [59:24] I remember when we first got under Joel and we were starting to go through, he made us create this gigantic sheet of nothing but numbers. And he goes, okay, Christy, tell me what the closing rate means. It means how they did? Well, how does this affect the person? Why is this important for you to actually know what the closing rate is? Why should you care?

 

[59:44] Which then actually leads us into the closing rate. So the closing rate is off of the appointments booked versus the sales closed. Now, this is, of course, mostly for inbound. You can do it for outbound, but it's going to be then more or less like the qualification calls.

 

[1:00:00] You can do it very similar to that. Like how many qualification calls led to close. So you could see on that kind of closing rate.

 

[1:00:10] This tells us that who's the top performer and it gives us that quick overview of, okay, so John has a 20% closing rate. Fantastic. But we have, you know, Jill who only has a 5%. Well, we got to figure out why does she have 5%?

 

[1:00:26] But we also then got to say, okay, well, if John has 12% or 15%, how can we get him even to 20%? Or even like Joel was saying about an extra percent.

 

[1:00:38] Because at the end of the day, like we said in part one, we want to get the middle people that are in the middle of the team that are not terrible and, you know, just need that extra push, get them to the front of the line. So then that way they're carrying a lot of the weight until we can get the people in the back caught up.

 

[1:00:56] So that gives us a general overview with that.

 

[1:01:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Calls Made Metric: The Diagnostic Starting Point"

 

NÚMEROS: 30 calls/day with only 1-2 connected calls, 60 calls/day target, 8 connected calls benchmark

 

VISUAL: Métrica de atividade de calls, diagnóstico de problemas de processo vs motivação

 

 

[1:01:02] But then we have the calls made. This one is where I always start to see where a problem is. Like how a doctor says, you know, how are you feeling? They always start there because they don't know, okay, you know, what exactly are you in for?

 

[1:01:18] Especially if you don't know. If you've never had it before, you don't know what's going to happen. That's when they'll go ahead and start like that. Well, how are you feeling? This is similar to that.

 

[1:01:30] That we take a look and it's like, okay, guys, so when we take a look at all the numbers, so let's say that your connected call volume, you're only getting one or two connected calls, but yet you're doing 30 calls a day.

 

[1:01:44] So why is it that you have only one connected call and then you're doing 30 calls? That seems very nonproductive.

 

[1:01:52] Now, having low activity doesn't necessarily mean they're not accurate closers or they're lacking skill. It could be very much of a process, but there could be some other things as well.

 

[1:02:06] Maybe that they're losing their drive. Maybe you didn't patch home like why having 60 calls a day was very important. That'll make them to stop and slow down because now they're thinking, why am I doing this? The wonderful question that stops everybody, right?

 

[1:02:24] Then we have some people that it's like, you know, closing is not really a priority. You know, I'm working. I'll get to it. I'll get to it. Or maybe they just plainly don't care, which unfortunately can happen that they get into a mundane routine.

 

[1:02:38] And maybe it's not because they actually don't care, but they're just in a rut. But then we have the high activity, which shows that they have high drive and that they're pushing for results.

 

[1:02:50] But that just means, okay, one problem is that they have high activity, but maybe they don't have high results. So they're trying, but now we have to dig deeper into the why.

 

[1:03:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Calls Connected: Productivity Measurement"

 

NÚMEROS: 8 connected calls/day = productive, 1 connected call = questionable productivity

 

VISUAL: Distinção entre atividade e produtividade, medição de eficácia real

 

 

[1:03:04] And I touched on this a little bit. The calls connected measures the activity for the productive part. So if you have eight connected calls in a day, okay, so you were pretty productive, especially if you only have a couple hours, you were doing productive first versus you only had one, then what were you really doing?

 

[1:03:24] Because then is it really working to get you over to the finish line or just something to do?

[1:08:04] No Exactly. Well, there's a lot of high level stuff that we as managers get to know. The only reason is so we can be fortune tellers. In a certain way, we're fortune tellers that we can see down that road. We can see, okay, if we go in direction X, it's going to lead us to Y.

 

[1:08:22] But there's also times that the manager has to protect the team by not telling them things, which is very, very important. Because even though there is that open communication that we're telling our closers, you know, hey, I want you guys to tell me what's on your mind so I can help you out.

 

[1:08:40] If we also tell them, like, I'm going to make a very, very extreme case. Like, let's say your boss came in to you one day and said, Christy, I hate to say this. Next Friday, we're going to have to shut the whole sales team down. It's just not working.

 

[1:08:56] I have then that opportunity to figure out, okay, what's the best way to figure this out? How can I prepare my team for this? The short answer, there's not really a whole lot of preparation you can do.

 

[1:09:08] But I also know we still have to make performance. So what ends up happening is as a manager, typically they will not tell you that we're on the chopping block for a day or two in advance.

 

[1:09:22] Not because they don't like you, not because, you know, they're trying to be sneaky. But what they're doing is if they told you that same day that, you guys, by the way, next week you're all getting fired. Start looking for another job. That's one positive of telling you.

 

[1:09:38] But how many of you would actually still perform at the highest level knowing that in two weeks it's for nothing? Like, how many of you can actually say, no, I still keep pushing? Right? Not many of us can say that unless you really like the job anyway, then you probably would.

 

[1:09:56] So, but that's the hard part as a manager is that some things that you know, yeah, I could. I could. But is it going to help my team or is it going to hurt my team? And I'm the barrier between helping or hurting.

 

[1:10:12] Great. Great conversations. You guys can hear me loud and clear, right, Discord? Just want to make sure because I took off the headset, came back. Let me know if you can hear me. Fantastic. Thank you.

 

[1:10:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Trust Capital Banking System"

 

NÚMEROS: 10 years relationship vs 1 mistake example, 5 million followers analogy

 

VISUAL: Conceito de banco de confiança, depósitos e saques de trust, gestão de relacionamentos

 

 

[1:10:28] So back from break, slightly about five minutes, slightly over, but we are back. I heard the interview. I know James calling it an interview, but I heard the question that Dana asked Christy. Those are great questions and great answers too.

 

[1:10:44] We'll say one thing before we jump right into the next part. Christy is so right. Like as a manager, again, we're not always the most popular group, popular person in the crowd and we shouldn't expect to be, right?

 

[1:11:00] We will always at times appear to be the bad cop, the bad person, the unrealistic person, right? The, you know, not understanding person. And that happens and we just got to be okay with it, right?

 

[1:11:14] And you know, how many of you remember the, actually I don't think this was actually here in Ultra High Ticket Closer or even Inner Circle, but how many of you remember or have heard of the trust capital? Like, you know, social capital, but have you heard of trust? Depositing trust?

 

[1:11:32] It's like the social capital concept, right? But, and I think this was at the HTM level. I'm pretty sure Seafood Dan taught this at the HTM level. I'm pretty sure. But, and I can't remember briefly what it is. Maybe James and Christy can help me out if you know exactly, but it kind of works like social capital.

 

[1:11:50] Like, it's like, for example, right? You go to the HTM, the high ticket millionaire level, the high ticket leader level. If you go to the bank, you know, you can't say, hey, I've got 5 million followers. Give me some money, right? Let me loan some money, right? It doesn't work that way, right?

 

[1:12:08] You know, it's like what Seafood explains. You've got to put in, add value first, add value to your 5 million fans, right? Add value, add value, and then you can convert the value into sales, right? Some of them will buy.

 

[1:12:22] Then you can deposit that money in the bank. So you have that withdrawal and deposit, right? You deposit value and you can convert that to cash sometimes, and then you can withdraw the cash, right?

 

[1:12:34] The same thing, the same concept happens with trust. Trust is the same way. And this is something I had to learn the hard way. Truly. And this is something that, you know, I'm glad I learned it now, but I definitely learned the hard way, which I want all of us to get it.

 

[1:12:50] And I think you can really get it through experiencing it. But then it's how it works, right? It's like the same. So when we meet someone, and you guys agree, right? It's easier to break trust than it is to build trust. I think we all know that.

 

[1:13:06] We can all agree that it's easy to mess up. It's easier to fuck up. Like, you know, you could trust someone for 10 years, but they do something wrong one time to you. Oh, man, it's done. That relationship is D-U-N done.

 

[1:13:22] By the way, that's not how done is spelt. Like, I'm just, that's what my drill sergeant used to say to me. It was so funny. He always said, D-U-N done. I'm like, what? That's not D-U-N. It's D-O-N-E. No, no, D-U-N done. Anyways, I digress.

 

[1:13:38] But yeah, so people, you know, I know you for 10 years, but you did this one thing to me. Therefore, I'm going to break up with you. We're done, right? It's like that. So trust is a really interesting thing.

 

[1:13:52] Now, especially in management, it's even tougher. Let's put it that way. Just imagine this scenario, okay? And this is why, like, you have to get people to deposit trust into your bank.

 

[1:14:06] Just imagine, right? We all got bank accounts for trust. Every day, I got to get Felix and James and Christy to deposit their trust into my bank account. I got to. I have to.

 

[1:14:20] Because, you know why? Here's why. Some days, I've got to be the mean ass. I've got to be the bad director. I've got to be the jerk, right? I've got to be just really aggressive.

 

[1:14:32] And guess what? If I don't have enough trust less in my bank, Felix, James, and Christy could say, you're a jerk. I don't want to work with you. I'm out of here. F off. And that happens.

 

[1:14:46] And like a lot of managers who are inexperienced, they don't know that. They don't do that. They don't think about that, right? They think they can just act the same every single day and expect people to like know and trust them, you know? Like, it happens.

 

[1:15:02] Now, the good thing about a trust bank is you need to build it up. Because like I said, there are times where you have to be the bad guy. And the same thing for the closers too. It's not just for the managers, right? It's for the closers.

 

[1:15:16] Like, you've got to build the trust with the sales team. You've got to let them put their trust in your bank. Because otherwise, you end up taking the trust every day. You keep yelling at them or you've got to be mean to them. You've got to be hard on them.

 

[1:15:32] And you do it not just because you want to be a jerk, but because sometimes the situation, like Christy explains, makes you the bad person. But you need to have the trust there enough. That way they don't go all overboard and they don't end up just all being destroyed and just quitting, right?

 

[1:15:50] Now, inexperienced managers that I've worked with, they don't have the trust bank. And then, unfortunately, right, they do too much of that bad cop or they just are just too aggressive and then they just lose their people, good people over time.

 

[1:16:06] And the beauty of having that trust, the Q as a trust bank in town, that's cool. The good thing about the trust bank, right, quote unquote trust bank, is that you can take it whenever, you can use it whenever you need it, right?

 

[1:16:22] And then like, you know, I can always go back to Felix and James and Christy like, you know, a couple days later and go, a couple weeks later and go, hey, you know what? I had to be really hard on you that time. And, you know, I appreciate that you're still here. And here's why I had to be hard on you.

 

[1:16:38] And I know you didn't like, I know you didn't like me being hard on you, but here's why. And with the trust bank, they're still around. And when you say that, then they actually built, it builds back the trust, right?

 

[1:16:52] Then they understand, oh, I see. Now I get why Joel did that or said that or thought that way. I didn't get it at first, but now I see it, right?

 

[1:17:02] And then the same thing, the same thing goes for the sales reps, right? Sometimes it just happens. They don't know why you're mad. Yeah, happy yelling. They don't know why you're yelling at them. They don't know why you're frustrated, but you know why. You just can't reveal it to them right away, right?

 

[1:17:18] And then when the situation gets solved, then you come back and say, well, here's why. And I appreciate you sticking by...

[1:19:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Money Time Goal Challenge - Carrot System"

 

NÚMEROS: $50 for goal 1 (15K + 1 sale per person), $100 for goal 2 (goal 1 + 1 HCC/HSC per team), $200 for goal 3 (goal 1 + 2 HCC/HSC individual)

 

VISUAL: Sistema de incentivos em três níveis, estrutura de bônus progressivo, recompensas da própria comissão do manager

 

 

[1:19:30] So let's go to the next example. Oh, this one. Wow. Wow. Take some time to read it. I love this one. This is actually a good carrot as well to give also that form of example. That's why I wanted to pick this one as well, even though we're talking about goals.

 

[1:19:48] So we went ahead and we were like, okay, we want to get. And so going down to money time, goal one. If we hit our target of 15K goal, everyone on the team must make one sale to contribute to the 15,000. We'll get $50.

 

[1:20:06] If we achieve the goal one, and if everyone on your team, because this is for the accountability teams, gets one HCC or HSC boom per person, I will give each of the team members a total of $100.

 

[1:20:22] Goal three, if we achieve goal one and you get two HCC or HSC booms just from yourself this week, each person that achieves this goal will get a total of $200 bonus.

 

[1:20:36] This challenge is for this week only. Let's see if we can hit those goals. If you're ready to do this and confirm you are happy with your team and we'll get rolling.

 

[1:20:48] I like that. Don't cheap out. It's for making 400. Keep in mind. Go ahead.

 

[1:20:56] I was just gonna say, keep in mind that with certain carrots, these are specifically out of my pocket. This is not TDL like, oh, here's your money. Go give your team a carrot. This is specifically just me out of my own commission that I'm saying, okay guys, if we all reach this goal together, I will give you part of what I already have.

 

[1:21:16] On top of already whatever commission they're making. So keep that in mind that carrots don't always have to be given to you by the company specifically. They can be out of your pocket, but make sure if you do that, you already get permission from your higher ups.

 

[1:21:32] I already had permission to do this before, which is why I am able to do that. Just kind of a disclosure right there.

 

[1:21:40] Okay. So how does this line up with the smart goals? Okay, I see some people typing. Goals for each team, yes. One, two, and three, yep. Easy communication with the team and expressive intent, yes, good.

 

[1:21:56] Why do you need the permission of higher ups if you pay it out of your own pocket? That's actually a very good question. I'll come back to that in just a second actually. No, that's a good, that's a really good question. That's a really good question. We'll get back to that.

 

[1:22:12] It appears to follow the outline and understandable. Yep, it fits all the categories. So it tells us, of course, what we're measuring, who is responsible for this. Now, even though I am the manager of the team, we have it broken down where there's now individual accountability and team accountability as well.

 

[1:22:32] So now they know who is actually responsible for reaching this goal. So we already had the goal of 15K. We all know that it's only for this week. This was actually taken on Tuesday, I believe. So it's like only for this week and of course clearly defines what will happen if they reach it.

 

[1:22:50] Okay, so it does fit all of them. Now, I know that for an outside perspective, dates would be more specific. Like if I was to put on this date to this date, that would be a way to improve this because I already had the conversation with my team.

 

[1:23:08] I was able to just put this week and they understood what I was talking about it.

 

[1:23:14] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Permission for Personal Incentives: Why It's Required"

 

NÚMEROS: $200 example amount

 

VISUAL: Razões legais e éticas para autorização de incentivos pessoais, proteção contra acusações de suborno

 

 

[1:23:16] So now to go back to Adam's question, the reason why you have to get permission to use your own money is because in some cases it's looked down upon because it can look as a form of bribery.

 

[1:23:30] However, if you're also using it as a carrot, they may go, well, we have something already in place for that. You don't have to use your own money. Sometimes they'll say, here, I'm gonna give you $200. You can give your team a carrot.

 

[1:23:44] Okay, so companies have their different policies with that. Some are like, this is okay. Some people are like, don't do that. If you need something, here, we'll give you something to help you out with that. So it comes down to preference. And if there's anything that's already in place, they can put that in charge.

 

[1:24:02] So the other reason too is not just the whole bribery favoritism thing, but if it's not authorized, so this is where, again, right? It goes up the chain of command, making sure everyone knows.

 

[1:24:16] Imagine if Christy never told me she was using her own money, okay? Let's just say, right? I don't know. I don't know. Dan doesn't know. No one knows. Only Christy knows, okay?

 

[1:24:28] And suddenly there was a complaint in her team about, let's not use, let's say not maybe, well, the bribery thing might be one that could be an issue, but let's say it's, let's just say it was the monies, right?

 

[1:24:42] The monies being transferred for whatever reason, the PayPal didn't go through, didn't send to the right person. This thing happens now. It's like, oh, now the closes, now this, Peter's got to send it to how Christy sent it to Andreas. He said it was a split of everyone, you know, whatever happens, right?

 

[1:24:58] And it's like talking about money, but the money is coming from, in essence, a private party, because even though Christy represents a company, it's not a company funds, then if that dispute happens and it gets brought all the way up, then I'd be like blindsided because if a complaint somehow makes it to support or makes it to higher up, then I'd be like, whoa, Christy, you should have told me this, right?

 

[1:25:22] And this is the communication between lower management, upper management, mid management, right? We've got to manage it all, always.

 

[1:25:30] And then the other important thing too to note is that in terms of anything with money, and this is why with the carrots, I think that's something I talked to Christy about too. I would say to keep things liability-free and to make sure there are no issues or disputes that arise because of money, let the company handle payments to the contractors, which are the closers, because they have a legal agreement between the contractor and the company, not an agreement between Christy and the contractor, right? Christy and the closers.

 

[1:26:02] And the company, they sign an agreement, so keep it simple in case anything ever happened, in case anything escalates, there is already a proper legal way to go about it, right?

 

[1:26:14] Now, if Christy is doing it out of her own goodwill, she's cleared it with me, you know, I've cleared it with everyone else, everything's good, yeah, great, right? And then, yeah, so that answers that. And-

 

[1:26:28] Because we wanted to, we actually wanted to do a whole other structure with this as far as the piggy bank method, kind of like how when you are in with a group of friends, everybody puts $10 into the pot, whoever wins gets the whole pot.

 

[1:26:42] We wanted to do that and it was like, ah, you know, no, no, that's not a good thing. Let's try to not do that. This is why it's important.

 

[1:26:50] Totally, totally advice against that because now you've got other people putting in their money. Imagine if that money disappears. Like just imagine, right? I mean, it may not happen, but if it does happen, now we got to deal with a mess that we didn't even have to have in the first place had we not done that idea.

 

[1:27:08] So some of the carrots got to think about, right? Like if it involves money, I always say, you know what? Just leave it to the company to deal with...

[1:42:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Should I NOT Automate? - The 3V Formula"

 

NÚMEROS: 3 V's framework

 

VISUAL: Framework de decisão sobre o que não automatizar: Volume, Variability, Value

 

 

[1:42:40] So in regards to that, you know, it seems like it would be like the best idea to automate everything. But there's one thing that you need to ask yourself. What should I not automate?

 

[1:42:54] So this is the 3V formula. This is, I found and was very inspired by this one. So I just wanted to share with you guys as well, too. And these are questions you can ask yourself, you know. Is this high volume? Is there a variability that occurs? And as well as how much value does this help me?

 

[1:43:14] And let's kind of put this into a quick picture. You know, let's say if it's a daily report. You might want to have the automation to be able to grab all the information you need. But I may want to deliver it myself.

 

[1:43:30] Now let me ask you guys a quick question. Why do you think it'd be smart if I were to deliver it myself instead of the automation to deliver the report?

 

[1:43:42] So you can coach. What else? That's a good one. This would be more for a team. Let's actually put this into perspective. What if I had to give it to Joel? Why would I want to have the automation give me the data, but I want to deliver it personally?

 

[1:43:58] Explain why, humanistic approach. It shows respect and implication. Those are pretty good answers. Also in this case too, I want to make sure it's right before it gets in his hands. Let's say the automation, there's a little bit of a quirk that happens.

 

[1:44:14] Yep, QA work. Christy, you're not supposed to give away the answer. No, yes, quality assurance work.

 

[1:44:24] So these are certain things where it's a one-off process. These are things that must be tied with a bow on top. Those would be items that you don't want to automate. So keep in mind, those are the three Vs and as well as one-off processes.

 

[1:44:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How Do I Automate? - Tony Stark Looking at New Element"
