[1:44:44] Now, how do I automate is going to be the next part. And I love this picture. How many guys like Marvel? How many guys like Avengers? I love this picture of Tony Stark looking at the new element he created. And he just looks at it and says, how did this happen? How did my dad teach me how to make an element?

 

[1:45:04] So this is the idea of, and you can feel this way too. You're just looking at it. It's like, well, how the hell do I do this?

 

[1:45:14] So when it comes down to the tools in place, let's say you're working with a business owner or influencer and they have the direct tools. And they say, you can use these items as well too. Maybe you can use your own software as well.

 

[1:45:30] But you want to start out with the end outcome in the beginning. You want to say, this is what I have and how do I get to there? You want to look at what kind of triggers are going to be happening.

 

[1:45:44] And it's a very logic yes, no base, which it is a black and white type of boring logic. If you are a computer programmer in certain cases too, when you look at code, it's like it works or it doesn't. So that's the kind of idea, very emeraldy.

 

[1:46:02] And then you want to build that bridge to be able to connect all of those sections. I don't have an example, but Felix actually pulls out his iPad and his Apple pen and he actually draws all the workflows by hand before it actually turns into the beautiful tree as you saw.

 

[1:46:22] I can actually add on to that as well, that when it actually turns into a bit of fun, because what you do with these workflows and the automation, and I know a lot of people that are programmers could probably also really get into this as well, that you literally have to think so simplistic a kindergartner knows what's happening.

 

[1:46:42] How many of you have actually heard the person that said, you know, oh my God, this computer will just not do what I want it to do. How many of you have heard that complaint, right? I keep hitting this button and it doesn't work.

 

[1:46:56] So who's stupid in this case? Who's doing it incorrectly, the computer or the human? Yeah, push the button harder. Or just throw it over and push it with a sledgehammer. Yeah, it'll be fine.

 

[1:47:10] Yeah, it's a user error because in the computer, it only knows basically a yes or no answer. Did this happen? Yes? Okay, do this. Did this happen? No? Don't do this.

 

[1:47:24] So if you can hit basically in the case of Felix, draw it out, give it to a five-year-old and go, what's happening? They should be able to tell you what's happening. That's how simplistic it gets. But we're always thinking so complicated.

 

[1:47:40] And that's the one thing I had to learn when doing these workflows is you have to take it literally. Okay, you pick up the foot. Well, first you got to bend the knee. You got to bend the knee. You can't even pick up the foot without bending the knee. Okay, well, my leg kind of comes in a little bit. So the leg has to come in a little bit.

 

[1:47:58] All right, well, if I'm going to move it forward, make sure you're leaning on your other foot. All right, all right. So then we're going to go ahead and lift it. Now we're going to have to quickly move. It's that one step at a time.

 

[1:48:12] Or that famous of how do you make a peanut butter and jelly sandwich? I love that example. Sounds like you're hungry, Christy. I am, but that's not the point.

 

[1:48:24] Because a lot of people are like, oh, you take two slices of bread, you put the peanut butter and jelly on, you smush it together. No. But you put the peanut butter and jelly on the outside, then you smush it together? Yeah...

 

[1:48:24] Because a lot of people are like, oh, you take two slices of bread, you put the peanut butter and jelly on, you smush it together. No. But you put the peanut butter and jelly on the outside, then you smush it together? Yeah...

 

[1:48:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Simplicity in Automation: The Kindergarten Test"

 

NÚMEROS: 5-year-old comprehension level

 

VISUAL: Conceito de simplicidade extrema em automação, teste de compreensão infantil

 

 

[1:48:40] So that's the level of detail. You have to literally break it down to the most basic, basic steps. And that's what makes automation work. When you try to skip steps or assume the computer knows what you mean, that's when things break.

 

[1:48:56] And I think that's a beautiful segue into showing some actual examples. So let me pull up some of the workflows that we actually use in the sales department.

 

[1:49:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real TDL Sales Workflow Examples"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Screenshots de workflows reais do HubSpot, árvores de decisão complexas

 

 

[1:49:12] So what you're looking at here is one of our actual workflows. Now, I know this looks complicated, and it is. But remember what Christy said - each individual step is incredibly simple. It's just yes or no questions stacked on top of each other.

 

[1:49:30] So let's break this down. The trigger here is when someone fills out a specific form on our website. That's it. That's the trigger. Very simple.

 

[1:49:42] Then the first decision point: Did they answer yes to question three? If yes, they go down this path. If no, they go down that path. See? Simple yes or no.

 

[1:49:56] Now, down this path, we're going to assign them to a specific team. We're going to send them a specific email sequence. We're going to notify the closer on Slack. We're going to create a deal in their pipeline. All of that happens automatically.

 

[1:50:14] And down the other path, completely different actions. Maybe they go to a different team. Maybe they get a different email sequence. Maybe we don't create a deal yet because they're not qualified.

 

[1:50:28] But the beauty is, once this is set up, I never have to think about it again. It just runs. 24/7. No human error. No forgetting. No "oh, I was busy and didn't get to it."

 

[1:50:44] So James, how much time would you say this one workflow saves per week?

 

[1:50:50] Oh, easily 10-15 hours per week. Because before we had this, someone had to manually check every form submission, read through their answers, decide which team they should go to, create the deal, send the email, notify the closer...

 

[1:51:08] And with, let's say we get 200 form submissions a week, that's 200 times you have to do this process. Even if each one only takes 5 minutes, that's... what, 16-17 hours? Just gone.

 

[1:51:24] Exactly. And that's just ONE workflow. We have dozens of these running simultaneously. So you can imagine the compound effect of time savings.

 

[1:51:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Automation ROI Calculation"

 

NÚMEROS: 200 form submissions/week, 5 minutes each manual, 16-17 hours saved/week, dozens of workflows running

 

VISUAL: Cálculo de ROI de automação, economia de tempo multiplicada por múltiplos workflows

 

 

[1:51:38] And this is why, as a manager, you need to be thinking about this constantly. Where are my people spending their time? Is it revenue-generating activity or not?

 

[1:51:52] And if it's not, can I automate it? Can I eliminate it? Can I delegate it to someone else? Can I systemize it in a way that takes less time?

 

[1:52:06] Because remember, every hour your closers spend on admin work is an hour they're not closing. And if your closer makes, let's say, $10,000 a month and they're spending 40% of their time on admin work, you're literally paying them $4,000 to do admin work.

 

[1:52:26] Does that make sense? Is that a good use of their skills? No. So automate it.

 

[1:52:34] Now, let's look at another example. This one is for lead rotation.

 

[1:52:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lead Rotation Automation System"

 

NÚMEROS: Round-robin distribution, real-time Slack notifications

 

VISUAL: Sistema de distribuição automática de leads em rotação, notificações instantâneas

 

 

[1:52:42] So what you're seeing here is when a new lead comes in, the system automatically checks: Who's online right now? Who has the least amount of leads currently? Who hasn't received a lead in the longest time?

 

[1:52:58] And based on those criteria, it assigns the lead to the appropriate closer. Instantly. The closer gets a Slack notification. The lead gets an automatic email. A task is created in the closer's calendar to follow up.

 

[1:53:14] All of that happens in literally 3 seconds. Versus before, when Felix or I would have to manually look at everyone's pipeline, figure out who should get the next lead, transfer it over, tell them about it...

 

[1:53:30] That could take 5-10 minutes per lead. And if we're getting 50 leads a day, that's hours of work. Every single day.

 

[1:53:42] So automation isn't just about saving time. It's also about speed to lead. Because we know that the faster you contact a lead, the higher your closing rate.

 

[1:53:56] If a lead fills out a form and they get contacted within 5 minutes versus 5 hours, your closing rate can go up by 10-20%. That's massive.

 

[1:54:10] So automation actually increases revenue, not just saves time. That's the key insight here.

 

[1:54:20] Exactly. And I want to emphasize something Christy said earlier about the B2C side. We also use automation to ensure data accuracy.

 

[1:54:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Deal Amount Automation for Data Accuracy"

 

NÚMEROS: Single selection = automatic amount population

 

VISUAL: Sistema de automação de valores de deals, eliminação de erro humano em entrada de dados

 

 

[1:54:34] Because when humans manually enter deal amounts, mistakes happen. Someone types $5,000 instead of $50,000. Or they forget to enter it at all. Or they use the wrong currency.

 

[1:54:50] But with automation, when a closer selects "High Ticket Closer Program" from a dropdown, the system automatically populates the deal amount as $7,000. No room for error.

 

[1:55:04] And this is crucial for reporting. Because if your data is wrong, your decisions are wrong. If I'm looking at revenue reports and half the deal amounts are incorrect, I can't make good decisions about hiring, about goals, about anything.

 

[1:55:22] So automation also ensures data integrity. Which is huge.

 

[1:55:30] Now, I want to talk about what NOT to automate. Because this is where a lot of managers make mistakes.

 

[1:55:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What NOT to Automate: The Human Touch"

 

NÚMEROS: 3V Formula review

 

VISUAL: Lista de atividades que requerem toque humano, quando automação é contraproducente

 

 

[1:55:42] So remember the 3V formula: Volume, Variability, Value. If something is low volume, high variability, or high value, you probably shouldn't automate it.

 

[1:55:58] For example, closing calls. Should we automate closing calls? No. That's high value, high variability. Every prospect is different. Every conversation is different. You need human intelligence there.

 

[1:56:14] What about coaching sessions? Should I automate my one-on-ones with my team? No. That's high value, low volume. I only do a few per week, but they're incredibly important. I need to be present for those.

 

[1:56:30] What about crisis management? If a closer comes to me and says, "Joel, I'm struggling. I'm thinking about quitting," should I have an automated response for that? Hell no. That requires immediate human attention.

 

[1:56:46] So you have to use judgment. Just because you CAN automate something doesn't mean you SHOULD.

 

[1:56:54] The rule of thumb I use is: If it requires emotional intelligence, don't automate it. If it requires critical thinking, don't automate it. If it's relationship-building, don't automate it.

 

[1:57:10] But if it's repetitive, if it's data entry, if it's routing, if it's notifications, if it's scheduling - automate the hell out of it.

 

[1:57:24] Exactly. And one more thing I want to add about automation is that it's not set-it-and-forget-it.

 

[1:57:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Automation Maintenance: The Ongoing Process"

 

NÚMEROS: Weekly reviews, monthly audits

 

VISUAL: Processo de manutenção de automações, necessidade de revisão contínua

 

 

[1:57:36] You have to constantly review your automations. Because things change. Your process changes. Your products change. Your team changes.

 

[1:57:50] So what we do is every week, Felix and I review all the automations that ran that week. Did anything break? Did anything not work as expected? Do we need to update anything?

 

[1:58:06] And then once a month, we do a full audit. We look at every single workflow and ask: Is this still serving us? Can we improve it? Should we eliminate it?

 

[1:58:20] Because sometimes you build an automation for a specific campaign or project, and then that project ends, but the automation is still running. So you need to clean that up.

 

[1:58:34] Or sometimes you realize, "Oh, this automation is actually causing more problems than it's solving." So you turn it off.

 

[1:58:44] Automation is a tool. It's not magic. You still need to manage it. You still need to maintain it. But when done right, it's incredibly powerful.

 

[1:58:58] Absolutely. So let me ask Discord: How many of you are now thinking differently about automation? How many of you see the potential here?

 

[1:59:12] Type in the chat if this section has been valuable for you. If you're seeing automation in a new light.

 

[1:59:24] Awesome. I see a lot of yeses. I see a lot of "mind blown" emojis. Love it. Love it.

 

[1:59:34] So before we wrap up, I want to do a quick summary of everything we covered tonight.

 

[1:59:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Part 2 Summary: Key Takeaways"

 

NÚMEROS: N/A

 

VISUAL: Slide de resumo final, principais aprendizados da aula

 

 

[1:59:44] So tonight, we covered a LOT. We talked about biases - affinity bias, confirmation bias, halo and horns effect. We talked about how to recognize them in yourself and in your team.

 

[2:00:00] We talked about goal-setting frameworks - OKRs, KPIs, MTOs. When to use each one. The difference between top-down and bottom-up approaches.

 

[2:00:14] We talked about metrics. What to track, why to track it, how to analyze it. We showed you real examples from our team.

 

[2:00:26] We talked about the trust bank. How to build trust with your team so you can have the hard conversations when you need to.

 

[2:00:38] We talked about the 12-month rule. How to develop your people so they can move up and move on. How to not hold them back out of insecurity.

 

[2:00:52] We talked about sales activities versus non-sales activities. How to maximize your team's time on revenue-generating work.

 

[2:01:04] And we talked about automation. How to think about it, what to automate, what not to automate, and how to maintain it.

 

[2:01:16] That's a LOT of content. And honestly, if you implement even 10% of what we covered tonight, you will see massive improvements in your team's performance.

 

[2:01:30] But the key is implementation. Don't just consume this information. Act on it.

 

[2:01:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Implementation Challenge"

 

NÚMEROS: 10% implementation target, 48 hours action window

 

VISUAL: Desafio de implementação, chamada para ação imediata

 

 

[2:01:42] So here's what I want you to do. Within the next 48 hours, pick ONE thing from tonight's class and implement it.

 

[2:01:54] Maybe it's setting up a trust bank with your team. Maybe it's reviewing your metrics and identifying what you should actually be tracking. Maybe it's creating your first automation workflow.

 

[2:02:10] Just pick one thing and do it. Because action creates momentum. And momentum creates results.

 

[2:02:22] And then once you've done that one thing, pick another thing. And another. And another. And over time, you'll transform your entire sales operation.

 

[2:02:36] But it starts with one action. Today. Right now.

 

[2:02:44] So who's committed to taking action? Type "I commit" in the chat if you're going to implement something from tonight's class.

 

[2:02:56] Awesome. I see a lot of "I commit" messages. I see a lot of fire emojis. Love it.

 

[2:03:06] Now, before we close out, Christy and James, any final words? Any last pieces of wisdom you want to share with the HTC family?

 

[2:03:18] Yeah, I just want to say, you know, being a sales manager is hard. It's one of the hardest roles in any company. You're caught between leadership and your team. You're responsible for results but you don't control all the variables.

 

[2:03:36] But it's also one of the most rewarding roles. Because you get to develop people. You get to watch them grow. You get to see them achieve things they never thought possible.

 

[2:03:52] And that's what makes it worth it. So if you're thinking about becoming a sales manager, or if you already are one and you're struggling, just know that it gets better. You get better. And the impact you can have is incredible.

 

[2:04:10] So keep going. Keep learning. Keep implementing. And you'll get there.

 

[2:04:18] Beautifully said, Christy. James?

 

[2:04:22] Yeah, I'll just add to that. You know, everything we taught tonight, these aren't just theories. These are things we've actually done. These are lessons we've learned the hard way.

 

[2:04:38] So you have the opportunity to learn from our mistakes without having to make them yourself. Take advantage of that.

 

[2:04:50] And remember, sales management is a skill. Just like closing is a skill. It can be learned. It can be developed. You're not born a great manager. You become one through practice and experience.

 

[2:05:06] So give yourself grace. Give yourself time. But also hold yourself accountable. Keep pushing yourself to get better every single day.

 

[2:05:20] And if you ever need help, reach out. We're here. The HTC community is here. You're not alone in this journey.

 

[2:05:32] Love it. Love it. Thank you, James. Thank you, Christy.

 

[2:05:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Manager Application - Now Open"

VISUAL: Anúncio de vagas abertas para sales manager, link de aplicação

[2:05:58] It's in the Inner Circle. Just look for the sales manager application post. Fill it out. Be honest. Be thorough. And we'll review it.

 

[2:06:12] Now, I will say, it's not for everyone. It's intense. It's demanding. You will be challenged. You will be pushed. You will be uncomfortable.

 

[2:06:26] But if you're willing to put in the work, if you're coachable, if you're committed, you will grow faster than you ever thought possible.

 

[2:06:40] So if that sounds like something you want, apply. We'd love to see your application.

[2:09:18] [FIM DA TRANSMISSÃO]

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO COMPLETO (PARTE 2 - SEÇÃO FINAL)

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS ADICIONAIS:

200 form submissions/semana processamento manual

5 minutos por submission manual

16-17 horas/semana economizadas por UM workflow

Dezenas de workflows rodando simultaneamente

3 segundos tempo de execução automática (lead assignment)

5-10 minutos por lead distribuição manual

50 leads/dia volume médio

10-20% aumento em closing rate com resposta em 5 min vs 5 horas

$10,000/mês salário médio de closer

40% tempo gasto em admin = $4,000 desperdiçados

$7,000 High Ticket Closer Program price (exemplo)

48 horas janela de implementação desafio

10% implementation target mínimo

Weekly automation reviews

Monthly full automation audits

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS ADICIONAIS:

Speed to lead optimization (5 min vs 5 horas)

Lead rotation criteria: online status, lead volume, time since last lead

Round-robin distribution system

Real-time Slack notification integration

Automatic email sequencing baseado em respostas

Deal pipeline auto-creation

Task automation em calendários

Data integrity através de automação

Dropdown selection = automatic deal amount population

Weekly automation review protocol

Monthly full audit process

Set-it-and-forget-it myth debunking

Automation maintenance as ongoing process

Campaign-specific automation cleanup

Problem-solving automation identification e desligamento

 

ESTRUTURA ADICIONAL:

Form submission → decision tree → path A ou B

Question 3 answer = branching logic

Team assignment automation

Email sequence differentiation por path

Qualification-based deal creation

Crisis management human-only protocol

One-on-one coaching non-automation rule

Emotional intelligence activities = human only

Critical thinking tasks = human only

Relationship-building = human only

Repetitive tasks = automate heavily

Data entry = full automation

Routing/notifications/scheduling = full automation

 

FILOSOFIA DE AUTOMAÇÃO:

Tony Stark element creation analogy

Start with end outcome first

Logic-based yes/no decision trees

Bridge building between sections

iPad + Apple Pen manual workflow design

Kindergarten comprehension test

5-year-old explanation requirement

User error vs computer error distinction

Peanut butter & jelly sandwich exercise

Step-by-step breakdown extremo

Bend knee before lift foot example

Computer only understands yes/no

Skipping steps = automation failure

 

QUANDO NÃO AUTOMATIZAR:

Low volume tasks

High variability situations

High value interactions

Closing calls (high value + variability)

Coaching sessions (high value + low volume)

Crisis management (requires immediate human attention)

Emotional intelligence requirements

Critical thinking needs

Relationship-building activities

One-off processes

Tasks requiring "bow on top" finishing

Quality assurance critical deliverables

 

ROI DE AUTOMAÇÃO:

Time savings compound effect

Not just saving time = increasing revenue

Speed to lead = higher closing rates

Data accuracy = better decisions

Hiring decisions dependent on accurate data

Goal setting dependent on accurate data

Reporting integrity crucial

Manual entry errors eliminated

Currency mistakes prevented

Missing data problems solved

 

IMPLEMENTAÇÃO E AÇÃO:

Pick ONE thing within 48 hours

Action creates momentum

Momentum creates results

One action leads to next

Transform entire operation over time

"I commit" accountability

Learn from others' mistakes

Sales management is learnable skill

Not born great = become great through practice

Give yourself grace + hold accountability

Push yourself daily

Community support available

HTC family não está sozinho

 

RECURSOS E PRÓXIMOS PASSOS:

Part 1 recordings available

Part 2 recordings available

Slides downloadable

Discord support ongoing

Tag team for questions

Sales Manager application open (Inner Circle)

12-month development program

Intense and demanding warning

Coachability requirement

Commitment requirement

Fast growth promise

Work directly with Joel/Christy/James

 

DURAÇÃO TOTAL: ~129 minutos (2h09min completo) FORMATO: Webinar ao vivo completo via Zoom/Discord CONCLUSÃO: Encerramento oficial da série Ultra High Ticket Closer EQUIPE: Joel Goh (Director), Christy Hinkson (B2C Manager), James Adamson (B2B Manager), Felix (mencionado), Dan Lok (Sifu)
